title: CHAPTER FOUR: BLOOD AND MOONLIGHT
content:
> *"The Thirteen Demonic Sects are not what the righteous factions would have you believe -- a collection of mindless butchers driven by bloodlust and depravity. They are organizations. They have logistics, intelligence networks, and strategic objectives. The Blade Sect, in particular, operates with a military discipline that would impress any general. Their leader has never been defeated in single combat. He has, on three occasions, fought masters of the Nine Great Sects to a standstill. He is not evil. He is something more dangerous: effective."*
>
> -- Desolate Sect intelligence briefing, classified, distributed to Division Commanders only

---

The new martial art came to him in pieces, like a puzzle assembling itself in the dark.

Yeon-shin locked himself in his room. Not the training ground -- this work was too dangerous for open space. If someone disturbed him while his energy was in flux, if a servant barged in or a brother decided today was the day for petty cruelty, the resulting disruption to his qi circulation could kill him.

*If someone disturbs me and my energy and blood flow get twisted, I'll die.*

He thought about his family, and the casual disregard with which they treated his existence.

*So I'll lock the door.*

He sat cross-legged on the floor, closed his eyes, and began to meditate.

Where the Dynamic Technique was movement -- the body as engine -- this was stillness. Receptive stillness, the kind that opens the practitioner to heaven and earth the way a cup opens to rain. Four-count inhale through the crown. Hold for two. Six-count exhale down the central channel.

The energy responded immediately. Of course it did. His Baihui acupoint was a gate thrown wide. Energy poured through him with an ease that would have taken other practitioners decades to achieve -- like drinking from a fire hose. The supply was not the problem. Control was the problem.

Hours passed. The morning light that leaked through his shuttered windows crawled across the floor, climbed the far wall, and faded. The shadows shifted. The sounds of the estate -- servants calling, livestock lowing, the distant clatter of the kitchen -- rose and fell like tides.

Yeon-shin sat. Breathed. Worked.

The energy would not compress. Would not layer. Would not densify. Like trying to stack water on top of water.

*Wrong approach. The energy doesn't need to be compressed -- it needs to be refined. The way you smelt iron into steel.*

But that realization, important as it was, was not what stopped him.

What stopped him was what he found at the crown of his skull.

The Baihui acupoint. The gate. He'd always known it was open. He had never looked at it this closely.

It was not merely open.

It was *growing*.

The aperture was wider than it had been a year ago. Significantly wider. Energy flowed through it in a torrent that would have reduced an ordinary practitioner to ash. The only reason it hadn't destroyed him was a decade of the Dynamic Technique tempering his body to handle the load.

For now.

*How long?*

He did the calculation with the cold precision of a boy who had spent his life converting pain into data. Compared the current flow rate against the aperture's expansion rate. Extrapolated.

Five years. Maybe less.

Five years before the gate opened so wide that no amount of physical tempering could contain the energy flooding through it. Five years before his upper dantian overloaded, his consciousness dissolved, and his body followed.

Five years before he ascended.

Not to heaven. Not to immortality. To death, dressed in the language of transcendence.

"Am I going to die like this?"

His voice sounded strange in the empty room. Small. Young. A fifteen-year-old boy sitting alone on a floor that needed sweeping, discovering that the gift that was the only interesting thing about his life was also the thing that would kill him.

*Die? Me?*

His hands were shaking. He watched them tremble -- these hands that had gripped a training sword since they were big enough to close around a hilt, that had created martial arts the way other boys created mischief.

The burning at the crown of his skull intensified. A sharp, searing pressure, as if someone were pressing a heated coin to the top of his head. His vision whited out -- just for a second, a flash of blank nothingness that swallowed the room -- and then it was back, the dim shapes of his furniture reforming around him.

*I need air.*

He stood. Crossed to the door. Turned the handle.

And stopped.

The smell hit him first. Copper and iron and something sweeter beneath -- the smell of opened bodies, thick as paint on the back of his throat. His stomach clenched once, hard.

Blood.

He grabbed his sword.

---

The estate was silent.

Not sleeping silence. After-silence. The held-breath quiet of a place where something has happened that cannot be taken back. He moved through the corridors, sword in hand, the Dynamic Technique flooding his muscles hard as iron and his senses sharp as glass.

No bodies. Not yet. The smell thickened as he moved toward the main buildings. No moon. No stars. Blackness so complete it felt solid.

He found them in the main courtyard.

The first thing he saw was the family head's banner -- a silk pennant embroidered with the Jeong family crest, torn from its pole and lying in something dark and wet. The second thing he saw was Aunt Lim, the first wife's sister, who had come for the banquet and was now lying face-up near the ornamental pond with a sword wound carved into her chest so precisely it looked like calligraphy.

The third thing he saw was all of them.

Bodies. A dozen, more, scattered across the courtyard. Family members. Servants. Guest disciples who had been reciting imperial history that afternoon. The blood had not formed rivers -- it had soaked into the packed earth, turning the ground dark and yielding, dripping from the thresholds in thin steady streams.

Every body bore the same wound. A single sword cut across the chest, identical in angle and depth. Training dummies.

The serving woman who had always left an extra rice cake on his tray lay crumpled against the garden wall. Her hand was open, reaching toward nothing.

*Ah.*

The sound that came out of Yeon-shin was not a word. It was not a gasp or a cry or a scream. It was a breath -- just a breath, a small exhalation that carried the weight of everything he would not allow himself to feel, not now, not here, not while the people who had done this might still be in the compound.

He shut it down. Grief, horror, nausea -- shoved into a room at the back of his mind, door slammed, the way he'd been slamming doors his entire life. What remained was clear and cold.

*Uniform wounds. Single attacker or a group trained to identical standards. Descending diagonal, right to left. Speed over power.*

He was analyzing while stepping over the body of a man who had served his family for twenty years. Analyzing because if he stopped he would feel, and if he felt he would scream, and if he screamed he would not stop.

**BOOM.**

The explosion came from outside the estate walls. A massive impact, sword energy against sword energy, the kind of collision that shook the ground and sent cracks racing through the courtyard stones. Formidable masters were fighting beyond the main gate.

And in the courtyard, ten figures sat in a loose circle near the main building, casual as travelers resting at a roadside inn.

Yeon-shin walked toward them.

He did not hurry. He did not try to be stealthy. He walked with the steady, unhurried pace of a boy who had just discovered that he was going to die in five years and had decided, in the space of a single terrible evening, that this simplified certain decisions considerably.

One of the figures turned. A large man, twin swords crossed on his back, with the scarred hands and dead eyes of a professional killer.

"Who are you?"

"The third son of this household."

The man stood. He was a head taller than Yeon-shin and twice as wide. He did not draw his swords. He simply walked over and swung his hand in a descending palm strike infused with internal energy -- the contemptuous blow of a grown warrior disciplining a child.

Yeon-shin's eyes flashed sky-blue.

Energy surged through his central channel and lit up his perception like lightning across a dark plain. He *saw* the strike -- the hand, the energy behind it, the path, the openings.

He moved.

Left hand caught the descending wrist. Right hand struck the back of the man's hand, hyper-extending the joint. Twisted, locked, held -- internal energy reinforcing muscle and sinew until the grip was absolute.

The man wrenched, pulled, shoved with his full weight. The boy's fingers did not budge.

Yeon-shin squeezed.

*Crack.*

The sound of large bones breaking. A scream.

Nine heads turned at once. Nine pairs of eyes widened.

"Let go!"

"You bastard--"

"Just a moment." Yeon-shin released the wrist. The man staggered back, cradling his ruined hand. Then -- past reason, past technique, past everything except the humiliation of being subdued by a child -- he drove his good fist forward in a Cauldron Lid Punch, concentrating internal energy until the fist seemed to double in size.

Yeon-shin's eyes flashed again.

And something in him *opened*.

Not the Baihui. Something deeper. Intention blazing through his body like scripture written in fire, energy flowing from right foot and left hand to right hand and left foot, overlapping, densifying. His left foot shattered the courtyard stone as he launched forward.

*Scripture of Overcoming the Origin.*

The name arrived complete. A martial art that broke through limits. That took the raw power flooding through his open gate and gave it *purpose*.

The burning at his crown flared white-hot. His vision tunneled to a corridor of light and motion. The iron sword cleared its scabbard--

Three heads left three bodies in a single horizontal arc.

Blood sprayed in a fine mist, catching the faint light. Yeon-shin was already past them, twisting, falling sideways through space like a snowflake caught in a gale.

A woman with red hair -- Blood Flame Sect crimson -- raised a crescent wheel to block. Her arm was still rising when the blue-tinged sword punched through her throat. He pulled the blade free. The woman collapsed forward, the crescent wheel clattering against stone with a clear, almost musical note.

Four dead. Six remaining.

His breathing had not changed.

*First kills. I should be feeling something.*

The emotions were locked behind that door and the door was holding. His body was steady and alive in a way it had never been. The new martial art hummed through him like a second heartbeat.

*The Scripture is burning me faster. Every time I create something new, it accelerates the condition.*

Filed away. Not thinking about it.

The remaining attackers scrambled to their feet. Among them, a thin-bodied swordsman with nervous eyes. And behind him, a giant.

The giant was the one who mattered. Enormous, wrapped in dark martial robes, with a physique like a bear that had learned to stand upright. Dense, controlled energy. Fox-clever eyes studying Yeon-shin with something that might have been respect.

"You've devoted your life to the swift sword," the giant said. "You have no other tricks. Those men were caught off guard." A pause. "Still. It's amazing. How can someone your age move like that?"

"Blood Flame Sect?" Yeon-shin asked, nodding toward the dead woman.

"Me? No. I am from the Blade Sect."

The Blade Sect. One of the Thirteen Demonic Sects.

"You're from the Blade Sect. But this is a Blood Flame operation."

"That's right."

"What are two of the Thirteen doing in a place like Xinye County?"

"None of your business."

"Then why are you answering every question?"

The giant laughed. Rough, like a saw hitting a knot. "Because it's a waste. A martial artist trained like you, at your age? That's rare. And watching a country boy talk like he knows the center of the martial world is... entertaining."

"A waste," Yeon-shin repeated. A sneer curved across his lips -- the deliberate, brittle expression of a boy who had learned that when you cannot hit someone stronger, you can at least make them angry enough to make a mistake. "You're probably just afraid of my maternal family. You rootless bastard."

*My maternal grandfather was the retired commander of the Desolate Sect's martial force. I have never met him. But you don't know that.*

His heart hammered. His face was stone.

"You crazy little--"

The giant growled. Actually growled, a sound from somewhere below the diaphragm, and his energy spiked in a way that made the hair on Yeon-shin's arms stand straight.

"You can't kill me," Yeon-shin said. "No matter what you do. It's the same."

**BANG.**

The main gate exploded inward. Door panels, iron bracings, fragments of stone flying in all directions, and tumbling through the wreckage -- Precipice Sword of the Southern Edge Sect, his crisp Taoist robes shredded, blood running from the corner of his mouth.

*Even him--?*

**THUD. THUD.**

Through the breach in the wall: Yeo Il-shin and Shadowless Fist. Both bleeding. Both battered. The elf elder -- the immortal sword cultivator whose energy had been the most refined thing Yeon-shin had ever sensed -- was missing the top half of her left ear, sheared away by a blade that had come close enough to kill and settled for disfigurement.

The blood ran down the side of her neck in a thin line, vivid against her luminous skin.

Yeon-shin stared. Something cracked in his understanding of the world.

Then the shadow came.

He entered through the broken wall the way an avalanche enters a valley. A middle-aged man of extraordinary size, carrying a sword that was more cleaver than blade. The energy rippling from its edge was not merely powerful. *Absolute.* The kind of force that does not compete with other forces but replaces them.

Everyone stopped. The air compressed.

The giant who had been talking to Yeon-shin hit the ground in a full prostration.

"Sect Leader!"

*The Blade Sect Leader. In person.*

Every survival instinct screamed at him to make himself small.

Instead, he ran. Forward.

The Scripture of Overcoming the Origin blazed through him. His left foot cracked the courtyard stone, launching him at the prostrate giant.

The iron blade punched through the man's back and out through his abdomen. A jerk. A cough of blood. Stillness.

Yeon-shin stood. One foot on the dying man's back. Sword still buried. He looked up at the Blade Sect Leader.

"Even now," Yeon-shin said.

Sky-blue lightning in his eyes. The headache screaming. The burning at his crown like a brand being pressed to his skull.

"Can you kill me?"
